reasoning: A 2 percentage point structural shift in the US labor market's occupational share within a few years is historically unprecedented without a cataclysmic localized shock. White-collar jobs grew by about 5 percentage points over 37 years (1985 to 2022). Dropping by 2 percentage points in 3 years (2023-2025) would require a relative shift of over 3 million jobs out of white-collar and into blue-collar/services. While recent headlines (e.g., in late 2024 and early 2025) emphasize a 'white-collar recession' and AI disruptions, these typically reflect a slowdown in hiring (job openings) and targeted tech/professional layoffs rather than massive permanent declines in total white-collar employment. Furthermore, blue-collar jobs are historically more cyclical, meaning a standard economic downturn would further typically increase the white-collar percentage as construction/manufacturing contract faster. Given we are already in April 2025 and no such massive 2% drop has been recorded or widely reported yet, the likelihood of this happening in the remaining quarters of 2025 is extremely low.
